acyl_cl-alcohol.name: Ester Formation
acyl_cl-alcohol.info: When an acyl chloride reacts with an alcohol, the chlorine atom is replaced by the alkoxy group (-OR) from the alcohol. A byproduct of hydrogen chloride (HCl) gas is produced, which often appears as white misty fumes.
acyl_cl-alcohol.fact: Unlike the reaction between a carboxylic acid and alcohol, this does not require an acid catalyst or heating. This reaction is exothermic and produces HCl gas.

acyl_cl-benzene.name: Friedel-Crafts Acylation
acyl_cl-benzene.info: Benzene reacts with an acyl chloride in the presence of a Lewis acid catalyst, typically aluminum chloride (AlCl₃). The chlorine atom is replaced by the benzene ring, and hydrogen chloride (HCl) is evolved as a byproduct.
acyl_cl-benzene.fact: Unlike many organic reactions that exist in an equilibrium, Friedel-Crafts acylation is essentially irreversible.

acyl_cl-ether.name: Ether Cleavage
acyl_cl-ether.info: The most common catalyst used for this transformation is zinc chloride (ZnCl_2), though other Lewis acids like AlCl_3 can also be used.
acyl_cl-ether.fact: This reaction is used to identify unknown ethers.

acyl_cl-phenol.name: Phenol Acylation
acyl_cl-phenol.info: When phenol reacts with an acyl chloride (such as ethanoyl chloride), the hydroxyl group (OH) of the phenol attacks the carbonyl carbon of the acyl chloride.Sodium Hydroxide converts phenol into the phenoxide ion (C₆H₅O-). The phenoxide ion is a much stronger nucleophile than neutral phenol, making the reaction significantly faster.
acyl_cl-phenol.fact: Usually performed at room temperature, though gentle heating may be used depending on the specific acyl chloride.

alcohol-alkene.name: Alkene Alcohol Addition
alcohol-alkene.info: The reaction between an alcohol and an alkene is a common method for synthesizing ethers. This process is known as alkoxylation or, more specifically, the acid-catalyzed addition of alcohols to alkenes.In the presence of a strong acid catalyst (H₂SO₄, H₃PO₄), the alcohol adds across the double bond of the alkene.
alcohol-alkene.fact: This is an electrophilic addition reaction following Markovnikov's rule.

alcohol-carboxylic_acid.name: Esterification
alcohol-carboxylic_acid.info: The reaction between a carboxylic acid and an alcohol is a classic organic transformation known as Fischer Esterification. It is a reversible, acid-catalyzed condensation reaction that produces an ester and water.
alcohol-carboxylic_acid.fact: Fischer esterification is reversible and reaches equilibrium.

alcohol-oxidation.name: Alcohol Oxidation
alcohol-oxidation.info: The oxidation of alcohols is a fundamental transformation in organic chemistry, where the carbon-oxygen (C-O) bond is converted into a carbon-oxygen double bond (C=O).
alcohol-oxidation.fact: Secondary alcohols are oxidized to ketones. Tertiary alcohols do not undergo oxidation under standard conditions.

alkane-halogen.name: Alkane Halogenation
alkane-halogen.info: Alkane halogenation is a free-radical substitution reaction where one or more hydrogen atoms in an alkane are replaced by halogen atoms (typically chlorine or bromine). This reaction is the primary way to convert relatively unreactive alkanes into more versatile haloalkanes.
alkane-halogen.fact: It requires UV light or high temperatures (around 300°C) to provide the activation energy needed to break the halogen-halogen bond.

alkene-carboxylic_acid.name: Alkene Carboxylic Acid Addition
alkene-carboxylic_acid.info: The reaction between an alkene and a carboxylic acid is known as hydro-alkoxyaddition. Similar to the reaction with alcohols, this process produces an ester, but it proceeds via the addition of the acid across the double bond of the alkene.
alkene-carboxylic_acid.fact: This is a useful alternative to Fischer esterification because it does not produce water as a byproduct, making the reaction atom-economical.

alkene-halogen.name: Alkene Halogenation
alkene-halogen.info: The reaction between an alkene and a halogen (Cl or Br) is an electrophilic addition reaction. It is a fundamental way to synthesize vicinal dihalides (compounds where two halogen atoms are attached to adjacent carbons).
alkene-halogen.fact: Unlike alkanes, alkenes do not require UV light for this reaction; it happens readily at room temperature because the pi bond is electron-rich and easily attacked.

alkene-reduction.name: Alkene Reduction
alkene-reduction.info: Alkene reduction, commonly known as catalytic hydrogenation, is the process of adding hydrogen (H₂) across a carbon-carbon double bond to form an alkane. This reaction is essential in both laboratory synthesis and industrial processes, such as the production of margarine from vegetable oils.
alkene-reduction.fact: This reaction is used in the food industry to make margarine.

alkyne-halogen.name: Alkyne Halogenation
alkyne-halogen.info: Alkyne halogenation is an electrophilic addition reaction where halogens (Cl₂ or Br₂) add across the triple bond. Because an alkyne has two pi bonds, the reaction can occur in two stages: first forming a dihaloalkene and then a tetrahaloalkane.
alkyne-halogen.fact: Terminal alkynes can be distinguished from internal ones.

alkyne-oxidation.name: Alkyne Hydration
alkyne-oxidation.info: The oxidation of alkynes involves breaking the carbon-carbon triple bond to form oxygen-containing functional groups. The outcome of the reaction depends heavily on whether you are using mild or strong oxidizing agents.

acyl_cl-amine.name: Amide Formation
acyl_cl-amine.info: The reaction between an amine and an acyl chloride is a rapid and vigorous process that forms an amide. This is a type of nucleophilic acyl substitution and is one of the most efficient ways to synthesize amides in the laboratory.
acyl_cl-amine.fact: This reaction is used in peptide synthesis.

amine-carboxylic_acid.name: Ammonium Salt Formation
amine-carboxylic_acid.info: The reaction between an acyl chloride and a carboxylic acid is a common method used to synthesize acid anhydrides. This is a nucleophilic acyl substitution reaction where the carboxylic acid acts as the nucleophile.
amine-carboxylic_acid.fact: This is the first step in amide formation.

benzene-halogen.name: Benzene Halogenation
benzene-halogen.info: Benzene halogenation is an electrophilic aromatic substitution (EAS) reaction. Unlike alkenes, benzene does not react with halogens under normal conditions because its delocalized pi system is exceptionally stable. To overcome this stability, a Lewis acid catalyst is required to generate a stronger electrophile.
benzene-halogen.fact: Benzene reacts with halogens using FeX₃ catalyst

benzene-nitro.name: Benzene Nitration
benzene-nitro.info: Benzene nitration is a classic example of electrophilic aromatic substitution (EAS). In this reaction, a nitro group (NO₂) replaces a hydrogen atom on the benzene ring. Because the benzene ring is so stable due to its aromaticity, it requires a very powerful electrophile—the nitronium ion (NO²+) to react.
benzene-nitro.fact: Nitration is the first step in making explosives like TNT.

carboxylic_acid-reduction.name: Carboxylic Acid Reduction
carboxylic_acid-reduction.info: The reduction of a carboxylic acid is a process that decreases the oxidation state of the carbonyl carbon, typically converting the acid into a primary alcohol. This is a relatively difficult transformation because the carboxylic acid group is resistant to many common reducing agents.
carboxylic_acid-reduction.fact: This reagent is powerful and reacts violently with water.

nitro-reduction.name: Nitro Reduction
nitro-reduction.info: The reduction of nitro compounds—most commonly nitrobenzene—is a vital reaction in organic chemistry used to synthesize primary amines (like aniline). This transformation can be achieved through several different methods depending on the desired scale and the presence of other functional groups in the molecule.
nitro-reduction.fact: This method is very clean as the only byproduct is water, and the catalyst can be filtered off and reused/

halogen-phenol.name: Phenol Halogenation
halogen-phenol.info: The halogenation of phenol is significantly faster and easier than the halogenation of benzene. This is because the hydroxyl group (OH) is a powerful activating group. The lone pair of electrons on the oxygen atom is delocalized into the benzene ring, greatly increasing the electron density and making the ring highly nucleophilic.
halogen-phenol.fact: This is a characteristic test for phenols.

halogen-sulfur.name: Sulfur Dioxide Chlorination
halogen-sulfur.info: The reaction between sulfur dioxide (SO₂) and halogens (X₂) is a significant chemical process used to produce sulfur-halogen compounds or to facilitate redox reactions. The outcome depends on whether the reaction occurs in the gas phase or in the presence of water.
halogen-sulfur.fact: This compound is used as a chlorinating agent.

oxidation-sulfur.name: Sulfur Dioxide Oxidation
oxidation-sulfur.info: The oxidation of sulfur dioxide (SO₂) involves the conversion of sulfur from the +4 oxidation state to the +6 oxidation state, primarily forming sulfur trioxide (SO₃). This reaction is the cornerstone of the industrial production of sulfuric acid and a key factor in environmental chemistry.
oxidation-sulfur.fact: This is the contact process for sulfuric acid production.

oxidation-reduction.name: Water Formation
oxidation-reduction.info: The formation of water from its constituent elements—hydrogen and oxygen—is one of the most fundamental reactions in chemistry. While the reaction is conceptually simple, it is highly energetic and involves a complex chain mechanism.
oxidation-reduction.fact: The reaction is highly exothermic, meaning it releases a significant amount of energy in the form of heat and light.

amine-halogen.name: Amine Halogenation
amine-halogen.info: The reaction between amines and halogens (like Cl₂, Br₂, I₂) is a process that replaces the hydrogen atoms on the nitrogen with halogen atoms. This produces a class of compounds known as N-haloamines.
amine-halogen.fact: If the reaction is not controlled, the HX produced can protonate the starting amine, making it unreactive. This is why a base is often added to neutralize the acid.

alkene-benzene.name: Friedel-Crafts Alkylation
alkene-benzene.info: The reaction between an alkene and benzene is a classic example of Friedel-Crafts Alkylation. While Friedel-Crafts reactions traditionally use alkyl halides, using an alkene is a more 'atom-economical' industrial method because it doesn't produce a halogen acid byproduct like HCl
alkene-benzene.fact: The most famous industrial use of this reaction is the synthesis of ethylbenzene.

amine-oxidation.name: Amine Oxidation
amine-oxidation.info: The oxidation of amines is a complex area of chemistry because the outcome varies significantly depending on the class of the amine (primary, secondary, or tertiary) and the oxidizing agent used. Unlike many other functional groups, amines can be oxidized at the nitrogen atom, the adjacent carbon, or both.
amine-oxidation.fact: In biological systems, specifically in the liver, amines (from amino acids) undergo oxidative deamination.

alkyne-benzene.name: Friedel-Crafts Alkylation
alkyne-benzene.info: When benzene reacts with an alkyne in the presence of a strong Lewis acid catalyst (like AlCl₃), the reaction can occur once to form an alkenylbenzene (a styrene derivative) or twice to form a diarylakane.
alkyne-benzene.fact: If excess benzene is used, the reaction often continues because the intermediate alkene is more reactive than the starting alkyne.

benzene-oxidation.name: Benzene Oxidation
benzene-oxidation.info: The oxidation of benzene is quite different from the oxidation of alkanes or alkenes. Because of its aromatic stability (resonance energy), the benzene ring is exceptionally resistant to common oxidizing agents like potassium permanganate (KMnO₄) or potassium dichromate (K₂Cr₂O₇), which would typically react with double bonds.However, under extreme conditions or using specific catalysts, benzene can be oxidized.
benzene-oxidation.fact: Maleic anhydride is used extensively in the production of polyester resins, alkyd resins, and agricultural chemicals.

benzene-reduction.name: Cyclohexane Formation
benzene-reduction.info: The reduction of benzene is the process of adding hydrogen atoms to the aromatic ring to saturate the double bonds. Because benzene is exceptionally stable due to its resonance energy, this reaction is more difficult to perform than the reduction of simple alkenes.
benzene-reduction.fact: Cyclohexane, a colorless liquid used as a solvent and as a precursor for making Nylon.

alkene-phenol.name: Friedel-Crafts Alkylation
alkene-phenol.info: The reaction between phenol and an alkene is a specialized form of Friedel-Crafts Alkylation. Because the hydroxyl group (OH) is a very powerful activating group, phenol is much more reactive than benzene. This allows the reaction to occur under milder conditions, though it is often more complex due to the potential for reaction at both the oxygen and the carbon ring.
alkene-phenol.fact: In the presence of an acid catalyst, the alkene is protonated to form a carbocation, which then acts as an electrophile.

acyl_cl-alkene.name: Kondakov Reaction
acyl_cl-alkene.info: The reaction between an acyl chloride and an alkene is a variation of the Friedel-Crafts reaction, often referred to as the Kondakov reaction or aliphatic acylation. It is used to synthesize β-halo ketones or α,β-unsaturated ketones.
acyl_cl-alkene.fact: Unlike the acylation of benzene, which is very common, the acylation of alkenes requires careful control to prevent the polymerisation of the alkene.

alkene-oxidation.name: Epoxidation (Formation of Oxiranes)
alkene-oxidation.info: The oxidation of alkenes is a versatile area of organic chemistry because the product depends entirely on the strength of the oxidizing agent and the specific reaction conditions. These reactions typically involve the addition of oxygen across the double bond or the complete cleavage of the bond.
alkene-oxidation.fact: An alkene can react with a peroxy acid (like mCPBA) to form a three-membered cyclic ether called an epoxide or oxirane.

alkane-oxidation.name: Combustion
alkane-oxidation.info: The oxidation of alkanes is the process by which they react with oxygen or oxidizing agents. Because alkanes are saturated hydrocarbons with strong C-H and C-C bonds, they are generally quite unreactive (hence their old name, paraffins, from the Latin parum affinis meaning 'little affinity').
alkane-oxidation.fact: This reaction is highly exothermic, which is why alkanes are the primary components of fuels like natural gas, gasoline, and diesel.

alkane-nitro.name: Nitration of Alkane
alkane-nitro.info: The nitration of alkanes is the process of replacing a hydrogen atom with a nitro group (NO₂). Unlike aromatic nitration (which is very common), alkane nitration is more difficult because alkanes are saturated and lack the electron-rich system of benzene.
alkane-nitro.fact: Alkane nitration is typically carried out in the vapor phase at high temperatures (400°C to 500°C). In these conditions, the alkane reacts with nitric acid (HNO₃) vapors.

acyl_cl-alkane.name: Acylation of Alkane
acyl_cl-alkane.info: The reaction between an acyl chloride (acid chloride) and an alkane is relatively rare compared to other acylation reactions. This is because alkanes are saturated hydrocarbons with very strong C-H bonds and no pi electrons to attack an electrophile.
acyl_cl-alkane.fact: In the presence of a powerful catalyst like Aluminum Chloride (AlCl₃), an acyl chloride can react with an alkane to produce a ketone.

alcohol-alkyne.name: Alkoxylation
alcohol-alkyne.info: The reaction between an alcohol and an alkyne is a nucleophilic addition reaction, often referred to as alkoxylation. Because alkynes are unsaturated, alcohols can add across the triple bond to form vinyl ethers or acetals.
alcohol-alkyne.fact: In the presence of a strong base (like KOH or sodium alkoxide), an alcohol adds to an alkyne to form a vinyl ether.

alkyne-phenol.name: Amine Oxidation
alkyne-phenol.info: The reaction between phenol and an alkyne is a specific type of nucleophilic addition known as vinylation. Because phenol has a nucleophilic hydroxyl group (OH), it can add across the triple bond of an alkyne to form aryl vinyl ethers. In the presence of a strong base such as potassium hydroxide (KOH) or an alkali metal phenoxide, phenol reacts with an alkyne (often acetylene) to produce a vinyl ether.
alkyne-phenol.fact: The base deprotonates the phenol to form a phenoxide ion (C₆H₅O-).

alkyne-amine.name: Hydroamination
alkyne-amine.info: The reaction between an alkyne and an amine is a nucleophilic addition reaction known as hydroamination. This process involves the addition of an N-H bond across a ≡ triple bond to form enamines or imines. When a primary or secondary amine reacts with an alkyne, the nitrogen atom attaches to one carbon of the triple bond, and the hydrogen atom attaches to the other.
alkyne-amine.fact: When the amine group and the alkyne group are on the same molecule, the reaction leads to the formation of nitrogen-containing heterocycles, such as pyrroles or piperidines.

alkyne-carboxylic_acid.name: Vinyl Ester Production
alkyne-carboxylic_acid.info: The reaction between an alkyne and a carboxylic acid is an addition reaction that typically produces vinyl esters. Because carboxylic acids are relatively weak nucleophiles, this reaction usually requires a catalyst—either a strong acid or, more commonly, a transition metal catalyst like Mercury (Hg), Gold (Au), or Ruthenium (Ru).
alkyne-carboxylic_acid.fact: The reaction generally follows Markovnikov's Rule, where the carboxylate group (R'COO-) attaches to the more substituted carbon of the alkyne.

acyl_cl-alkyne.name: Electrophilic Addition
acyl_cl-alkyne.info: The reaction between an alkyne and an acyl chloride is an electrophilic addition reaction. Similar to the acylation of alkenes, this process is used to synthesize β-halo-enones or α,β-unsaturated ketones. Because alkynes have a higher degree of unsaturation, the reaction can be controlled to stop after a single addition, providing a functionalized alkene as the product.
acyl_cl-alkyne.fact: In the presence of a Lewis acid catalyst, the acyl group (RCO-) and a chlorine atom add across the triple bond.

alkyne-reduction.name: Complete Reduction to Alkanes
alkyne-reduction.info: If an alkyne is treated with hydrogen gas in the presence of a standard metal catalyst, it will be fully reduced to an alkane. It is very difficult to stop this reaction at the alkene stage using these catalysts because alkenes are generally more reactive toward hydrogenation than alkynes.
alkyne-reduction.fact: Hydrogenation works normally to produce 1-alkenes or alkanes.

alcohol-halogen.name: Oxidation by Halogen
alcohol-halogen.info: Halogens, particularly Chlorine (Cl₂) and Bromine (Br₂), act as oxidizing agents toward alcohols. This reaction typically occurs in the presence of water or under alkaline conditions.
alcohol-halogen.fact: Secondary Alcohols are oxidized to ketones.Tertiary Alcohols: Are generally resistant to oxidation by halogens because they lack a hydrogen atom on the carbinol carbon (the carbon attached to the -OH group).

carboxylic_acid-halogen.name: Hell-Volhard-Zelinsky (HVK) Reaction
carboxylic_acid-halogen.formula: R-CH₂-COOH + X₂/Red P + H₂O → R-CH(X)-COOH + HX
carboxylic_acid-halogen.info: The Red Phosphorus reacts with the halogen to form PX₃, which converts a small amount of the acid into an acid halide. The acid halide exists in equilibrium with its enol form, which is then halogenated at the α-position. Finally, the halogenated acid halide reacts with more carboxylic acid to regenerate the catalyst and produce the final product.
carboxylic_acid-halogen.fact: The product formed is an α-halo carboxyllic acid

ether-halogen.name: Halogenation
ether-halogen.info: The reaction between halogens and ethers typically involves the substitution of hydrogen atoms on the carbon chains. Ethers are generally quite stable, but in the presence of light or heat, they can be halogenated via a free-radical mechanism.
ether-halogen.fact: In the absence of light, the reaction is slow and typically only replaces the hydrogen atoms on the α-carbons (the carbons directly attached to the oxygen atom).

acyl_cl-halogen.name: Alpha-Halogenation of Acyl Chloride
acyl_cl-halogen.info: Acyl chlorides are more reactive than carboxylic acids toward α-halogenation because they exist in a more significant equilibrium with their enol form.
acyl_cl-halogen.fact: The product formed is an α-halo acyl chloride.

halogen-oxidation.name: Oxidation of Halogen
halogen-oxidation.info: When halogens react with water or alkalis, they often undergo disproportionation—a reaction where the same element is simultaneously oxidized and reduced.
halogen-oxidation.fact: Except for Fluorine, halogens can exist in positive oxidation states (+1, +3, +5, +7) when combined with more electronegative elements like Oxygen or Fluorine.

halogen-reduction.name: Hydrogenation of Halogen
halogen-reduction.info: The hydrogenation of halogens is a fundamental redox reaction where a halogen molecule (X₂) reacts with hydrogen gas (H₂) to form a hydrogen halide (HX).
halogen-reduction.fact: The reaction is a direct combination (synthesis) reaction

oxidation-phenol.name: Air Oxidation
oxidation-phenol.info: The oxidation of phenol is more complex than that of simple aliphatic alcohols. Because the hydroxyl group is attached to an aromatic ring, the reaction often affects the ring itself rather than just the oxygen atom.
oxidation-phenol.fact: Pure phenol is a colorless crystalline solid, but it often turns pink or reddish-brown over time.

alcohol-reduction.name: Reduction to Alkanes
alcohol-reduction.info: The most direct way to reduce an alcohol to an alkane involves using Hydroiodic Acid (HI) in the presence of Red Phosphorus. This is a powerful reducing system capable of stripping oxygen from many functional groups.
alcohol-reduction.fact: This is often used in the reduction of complex sugars or polyols to their corresponding hydrocarbons.

phenol-reduction.name: Deoxygenation
phenol-reduction.info: This is the most common laboratory method for removing the hydroxyl group from phenol. It involves heating phenol with Zinc dust.
phenol-reduction.fact: This is a surface reaction where the Zinc acts as a reducing agent, stripping the oxygen from the phenol.

carboxylic_acid-phenol.name: Fishcher Esterification
carboxylic_acid-phenol.info: The reaction between phenol and carboxylic acids is a bit unique because phenols are less nucleophilic than simple aliphatic alcohols. Consequently, a direct reaction to form an ester (esterification) is often slow or requires specific conditions and catalysts.
carboxylic_acid-phenol.fact: The Product formed is an Aryl Ester (Phenyl Alkanoate)

nitro-phenol.name: Phenol Nitration
nitro-phenol.info: When phenol reacts with dilute HNO₃ at a low temperature (room temperature, approximately 298 K), it produces a mixture of ortho and para nitrophenols.
nitro-phenol.fact: The ortho isomer is typically formed in higher amounts but can be separated from the para isomer via steam distillation.

reduction-sulfur.name: Reduction of Sulfuric Acid
reduction-sulfur.info: Concentrated sulfuric acid is a strong oxidizing agent and is easily reduced by various substances, especially halides and metals.
reduction-sulfur.fact: Iodide is a stronger reducing agent and can reduce it all the way to hydrogen sulfide (H₂S)

benzene-sulfur.name: Benzene Sulphonation
benzene-sulfur.info: Benzene reacts with fuming sulphuric acid (oleum) or concentrated sulphuric acid at elevated temperatures to produce benzene sulphonic acid.
benzene-sulfur.fact: The actual electrophile is Sulfur Trioxide SO₃. Even in concentrated sulphuric acid, SO₃ is produced through the equilibrium

amine-sulfur.name: Amine Sulphonation
amine-sulfur.info: The sulphonation of amines is a significant reaction in organic chemistry, primarily used to produce sulphonamides, which are the basis for a large class of antibiotics (sulfa drugs).
amine-sulfur.fact: Aniline reacts with concentrated H₂SO₄ to form anilinium hydrogen sulphate.

ether-oxidation.name: Peroxide Formation
ether-oxidation.info: When ethers are exposed to air (oxygen) and light over long periods, they slowly react with oxygen to form hydroperoxides and dialkyl peroxides. This is a free-radical chain reaction
ether-oxidation.fact: Laboratories ether bottles are often tested for peroxides before use, beacause they are highly explosive and can detonate if the ether is distilled to dryness or heated.

acyl_cl-oxidation.name: Hydration
acyl_cl-oxidation.info: Acyl chlorides are generally resistant to common oxidizing agents like Potassium Permanganate (KMnO₄) or Potassium Dichromate (K₂Cr₂O₇) in anhydrous conditions. However, if water is present (which is common in many oxidation reactions), the acyl chloride will first undergo hydrolysis to form a carboxylic acid.
acyl_cl-oxidation.fact: Any further oxidation would then act upon the resulting carboxylic acid (e.g., oxidizing a side chain).

acyl_cl-reduction.name: Reduction to Primary Acid
acyl_cl-reduction.info: The first hydride displaces the chloride ion to form an aldehyde. Because the aldehyde is more reactive than the starting acyl chloride, a second hydride immediately attacks to form a primary alcohol.
acyl_cl-reduction.fact: If you want to fully reduce the carbonyl group, strong hydride donors are used.

acyl_cl-carboxylic_acid.name: Acid Anhydride Synthesis
acyl_cl-carboxylic_acid.info: When an acyl chloride reacts with a carboxylic acid, the hydroxyl oxygen of the acid acts as a nucleophile, attacking the carbonyl carbon of the acyl chloride.
acyl_cl-carboxylic_acid.fact: Pyridine is typically added to the reaction mixture; It acts as a base to neutralize the HCl gas produced, and it acts as a nucleophilic catalyst.

alkene-sulfur.name: Alkene Sulphonation
alkene-sulfur.info: When an alkene reacts with cold, concentrated sulphuric acid, it undergoes an addition reaction to form an alkyl hydrogen sulphate.
alkene-sulfur.fact: In industrial chemistry, this reaction is fundamental for producing Alpha-Olefin Sulphonates (AOS), which are widely used as biodegradable detergents and surfactants.
